author: @woodworks2123
text: Apart from the obvious waste of water, this does open some options. You cant drive it directly, what if you topped up a water tank up high either in your loft or if your remote a home  water tower then once it's full, a valve is opened then this drives a turbine, how long it would take to empty would have to calculate on a few parameters but it's basically a home version of pumped hydro. It's only wasteful if the water just goes down the drain, if you were to use it to water the garden, fill toilets etc it isn't wasteful at all.

author: @monkeyfootracing645
text:
I've been thinking about something Ike this. 
My water comes from a spring in a 1inch line to the cistern and runs all year long. 
It will end up with 3. One on the spring line, one on the output of the cistern and one on the cistern overflow. I plan to charge a battery and periodically use that to pump water 300 feet up to the garden. The spring head is not suitable for a ram pump so I will try to use the battery pump  once a fortnight or so.

author: @toml.8210

European royalty used these principles to power their  water fountains.
They put a big pipe in a fast river near their palace, reduced the pipe diameter to increase pressure, then had it squirting water everywhere! (The  Versailles water gardens.)

The challenge would be - to make a small 'paddle style' generator to take advantage of the water flow - as a continuous source to charge up storage batteries.

author: @breaking_bear
text: I'd be curious if an experiment can be done to prove or disprove the loss of pressure from adding the water generator to the water line. I'd wager the law was created more for the power companies, so folks can't collect free power. Fitting name "power companies". And if so, how many people can do it before it becomes dangerous. How many psi is really necessary before ground water enters the pipes? Can ground water even enter water pipes? I'd wager not. I'd say they're likely sealed pretty tight.

author: @Unpopular_0pinion
text: It's crazy to me the logic of making it illegal to hook these up to the main line. If everyone installed them to their main line, wouldn't it increase the pressure in the municipal water supply because of all the restricted flow? In some ways you'd think it would save the town money. Sure you'd have to worry about the people that would run their water explicitly to generate electricity using the towns supply.. but you have that same problem currently with it being legal to hook it up at a secondary location with a bs reason for usage. Am I missing something or does this seem like a pointless regulation in this day and age?

author: @andrewallason4530
text:
G’day Robert from far off Tasmania.  I’ve been thinking of a solar power steam engine using methanol ( boiling point of 66’c).  My concept is a solar unit heating a reservoir of low viscosity oil ( high flash point, able to hold much higher temperatures than water).  A heat exchanger heats the methanol, generating steam, turning a Uniflo type steam engine geared onto a generator.  
We routinely get temperatures over 80’c in our solar hot water system, here in Tasmania, even on cooler winter days.  
I figure the solar evacuated-tube (aluminium tubes, partially flattened to form ‘strips’, painted black, inside a glass vacuum tube) would be  more than capable of heating the oil.

author: @shawnduddridge
text:
Gday Rob, im wanting to build an electric boat where the motors on the back double as tidal & wind generators.   Would love to hear some feedback from either yourself or a skilled viewer on how best to acheive this, or any ideas on it at all.
I was going to use trolling / fishing motors as theyre already quite sleek and fully waterproofed.  And realise i would probably have to switch propellors, or add shrouds etc.. to acheive decent performance in all 3 areas.  Or could there be a 1 size fits all prop for all 3? The motors would be 80lbs thrust so theyre fairly low speed & 24V DC.
Any thoughts from anyone would be great, thanks!

author: @willlockler9433
text: Got me thinking. Instead of Pelton cups, what about a group of helix blades on the rotor. Think helical gear for a visual. The exhaust would be 90 degrees to the input, coaxial to the rotor shaft. Energy extraction across the entire width of the rotor, less turbulence in the chamber, smoother flow altogether. The input would be positioned on the opposite end of the rotor housing from the exhaust, still at 90 degrees to the shaft. Just wondering.

author: @401ksolar
text: I installed some water meters for a trailer park once that used a similar principle to drive a physical counter, the older ones used a wheel that the water pelted to make it spin, it turns out no 2  meters had the same reading so the park updated to a positive displacement motor which used rollers on a rotor that pressed into a flexible tubing trapping a fixed amount of fluid per rotation quite similar to a pump in a dialysis machine, I wonder if the accuracy change would equal increased torque just by the nature of positive displacement Theory?

replies.author: @401ksolar
replies.text: oddjobbob yes that type of positive displacement motor works very well in a low velocity environment, I'm thinking in order to achieve accuracy with a pelted wheel without losses the velocity would have to be so great as to create a fluid seal between the housing and the rotor, the same way a hydraulic seal works in a centrifugal water pump

author: @lohikarhu734
text: I guess that any application that has a zero outlet pressure, like pond, swimming pool, toilet, any of these could readily use a 'water motor' to provide a few watts, or more, to power, say, a sprinkler timer/valve, an IOT monitor for weather, soil moisture, yard motion/video monitoring... I was thinking that a positive displacement motor would extract the most power, but perhaps at a lower RPM than a 'turbine' type ...

author: @deadpoet4
text: Leaks galore!  Unless you do the rotor with magnets as part of the sealed section.  Same blades and pipes in center with blades directly attached to a plate with magnets on the rim.  Whole assembly in a sealed  case, with coil on outside of case around the rotor plate.  Since nothing needs to move outside the container, no leaks!

author: @kadmow
text: ha ha - don't use with steam if printing with PLA, PETG... - even ABS if it is "significantly" pressurised (superheated -at all ie. only ~20kPa, 2PSI ) - PLA, which many people print with will enter its plastic range - ie. the glass transition temp is only ~60-65 degrees C  (PETG 85C)- even ABS transitions to plastic at around 105 degrees  C...   -  PEEK transitions at 145deg C - but is a bit fiddly to print with (very hot hotend temps (365-440Deg C) and high chamber temps (135 or something are needed...

replies.author: @marcfruchtman9473
replies.text: While I truly admire the ingenuity, the idea of using water from the spigot or hose to generate electricity doesn't include the cost of the water for say 1 hour -- Assume somewhere around 2.5 gallons per minute thru a short hose at modest flow and that's over 100 gallons of water for 1 hour. In the USA, 100 gals of water will cost the average person  at least 1 dollar.... I guess what I am saying is this: For 1 dollar you can have 10 kWh of electricity or... you can use a water generator to run an LED for an hour. So, when thinking about these ideas, remember that there is a "cost" do doing different things. And it is important to weigh those costs. If the water supply comes from a rain spout, it might be useful. But again, we had this discussion earlier... how much electricity are you really generating with a rain spout... I honestly believe it will "never" be worth the money you spend for the average consumer.

replies.author: @staycurious8650
replies.text: @marcfruchtman9473 Obviously not for use just charging a phone😅. Meant for use as harvester charging a battery when ever flow occurs in pipe. And I thought that was obvious.
